column1:
The difference in systolic pressure between two adjacent levels in the same leg should be no more than ___________mmHg.

A: 15
B: 20
C: 25
D: 30
column2:
Answer: D. 30

Feedback:
A drop in pressure of 30mmHg or more between segments indicates disease between the two segments.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes the symptoms associated with median arcuate ligament syndrome?

A: abdominal pain that is relieved by deep inspiration
B: abdominal pain that is relieved by eating a fatty meal
C: groin pain that is relieved by placing the legs in the dependent position
D: calf pain relieved by stretching the calf muscles
column2:
Answer: A. abdominal pain that is relieved by deep inspiration

Feedback:
The median arcuate ligament compresses the celiac axis on expiration. When the diaphragm moves cephalad, the artery is pinched, and abdominal symptoms occur. When the patient inhales, the diaphragm moves caudal, and symptoms are relieved.

column1:
The waveform from an abdominal vessel displays what two characteristics?

A: flow reversal during diastole
B: triphasic flow and no respiratory variation
C: mild phasicity and pulsatility
D: flow reversal during augmentation
column2:
Answer: C. mild phasicity and pulsatility

Feedback:
The waveform displays mild respiratory variation (phasicity) and pulsatility in a normal renal vein. Please note that the definition of pulsatility indicates regular variations in flow. Not all pulsatility in venous flow is a result of the ripple effects of cardiac contraction or cardiac pulsatility. These findings are considered normal in renal vessels. If venous congestion occurs, the flow will become increasingly pulsatile and flow reversal can develop during atrial contraction.

column1:
How can a Sonographer correct for blooming on a spectral Doppler display?

A: decrease the color Doppler velocity scale
B: increase the color Doppler velocity scale
C: turn the Doppler gain down
D: reduce the wall filter
column2:
Answer: C. turn the Doppler gain down

Feedback:
Blooming or blossoming describes artifactual echoes displayed around a waveform shape. It is caused by over-gaining and can lead to overestimation of the peak velocity. Properly adjusted gain will demonstrate a waveform of medium brightness and a clear spectral window(if present).

column1:
According to the Appropriate Use Criteria, which of the following is NOT a valid exam indication for a duplex exam on a hemodialysis fistula?

A: palpable thrill at the fistula site
B: 1 month follow up baseline exam post-intervention
C: Inability to cannulate at dialysis
D: ipsilateral arm swelling
column2:
Answer: A. palpable thrill at the fistula site

Feedback:
Hemodialysis fistulas and grafts will normally demonstrate a thrill over the connection site in the arm. Absence of the thrill can indicate an occlusion of the fistula/graft.

column1:
Which of the following can be used to eliminate aliasing seen on a CW Doppler tracing?

A: adjust baseline, increase scale and decrease probe frequency
B: decrease probe frequency
C: increase the scale
D: none of the above
column2:
Answer: D. none of the above

Feedback:
Aliasing does not occur with a CW probe. The beam is not pulsed, so it is not limited by the pulse repetition frequency/Nyquist limit.

column1:
A patient presents for a follow up exam after radiofrequency ablation of the GSV. What is the purpose of the exam?

A: To assess the valvular motion in the GSV
B: To confirm the contraction of the GSV and absence of thrombus in the deep system
C: To confirm the patency of the GSV and thrombosis of its branches
D: To evaluate thrombus formation in the GSV
column2:
Answer: B. To confirm the contraction of the GSV and absence of thrombus in the deep system

Feedback:
A follow up exam after radiofrequency ablation of the GSV should be performed 2 weeks after the procedure to confirm contraction/occlusion of the GSV and absence of thrombus in the deep system. Measuring the distance from the treated GSV segment to the SFJ is important to document. The fibrosis/thrombosis of the treated vein should not extend into the deep veins. Compression views of the treated veins and Doppler demonstration of the absence of flow should also be documented.

column1:
The Scimitar sign is associated with:

A: Bovine arch
B: Median arcuate ligament syndrome
C: Adventitial cysts
D: Femoral to Femoral bypass graft
column2:
Answer: C. Adventitial cysts

Feedback:
The Scimitar sign is associated with adventitial cyst formation. The arterial lumen is compressed by the cyst and is said to look like a scimitar (type of curved sword).

column1:
What is the most common cause of cerebrovascular disease?

A: atherosclerosis
B: embolism
C: stroke
D: vasospasm
column2:
Answer: A. atherosclerosis

Feedback:
Atherosclerosis formation is the most common cause of cerebrovascular disease. Embolism formation is much less common and is usually a complication of atherosclerosis formation. A stroke is a potential complication with all types of cerebrovascular disease.

column1:
What is the minimum amount of diameter stenosis in the internal carotid artery that will demonstrate hemodynamic significance?

A: 25%
B: 95%
C: 50%
D: 75%
column2:
Answer: C. 50%

Feedback:
Hemodynamic significance refers to the appearance of signs and symptoms related to the compromised blood flow. 50% diameter or 75% area stenosis of a vessel is described as hemodynamically significant.

column1:
You perform a carotid Doppler exam that demonstrates minimal atheroma formation bilaterally. You record waveforms that demonstrate laminar flow with the following velocities: Right Proximal CCA PSV 145cm/s, EDV 35cm/s Right Proximal ICA PSV 185cm/s, EDV 50cm/s Left Proximal CCA PSV 140cm/s, EDV 38cm/s Left Proximal ICA PSV 190cm/s, EDV 40cm/s What will you report on your worksheet?

A: Flow velocities are normal bilaterally
B: Flow velocities are increased bilaterally, which is most likely related to stenosis at the aortic valve
C: Flow velocities are increased bilaterally, which is most likely related to soft plaque formation not visible on 2D and color evaluation
D: Flow velocities are increased bilaterally, which is most likely related to increased cardiac output
column2:
Answer: D. Flow velocities are increased bilaterally, which is most likely related to increased cardiac output

Feedback:
Bilateral increased velocities in the CCA and ICA with no evidence of stenosis is usually related to increased cardiac output. Bilateral CCA velocities can be increased with aortic stenosis also, but the flow would not be laminar in the proximal CCA.

column1:
Which of the following is an abnormal finding for a hemodialysis graft?

A: inflow artery demonstrates a triphasic waveform
B: volume flow rate 500 - 1000ml/min
C: turbulent flow at the anastomosis site
D: high velocity, pulsatile flow in the outflow vein
column2:
Answer: A. inflow artery demonstrates a triphasic waveform

Feedback:
Normal flow velocity within the graft should be >100cm/s. Graft stenosis suspected with velocities greater than 400cm/s. Abnormal flow volume <500ml/min. If the flow velocity doubles (or more) between two points in the graft, significant stenosis is suspected. If the inflow artery demonstrates a triphasic waveform, graft occlusion is suspected. Turbulent flow is normal at the anastomosis site and the venous outflow will also be turbulent and pulsatile.

column1:
A segmental pressure exam demonstrates the following pressures: right arm: 142mmHg right leg: proximal thigh 140mmHg distal thigh 126mmHg calf 116mmHg ankle 112mmHg. Which of the following could explain these results?

A: infrapopliteal disease
B: pelvic mass
C: pelvic mass or mid femoral artery stenosis
D: medial calcification
column2:
Answer: B. pelvic mass

Feedback:
Using the four-cuff method, the proximal thigh pressure should be 30-40mmHg higher than the brachial pressure. The pressures are decreased in the entire leg at small intervals from the thigh down. This indicates aortoiliac disease or possible intrinsic compression of the iliac artery by a pelvic mass.

column1:
Rest pain is associated with ankle pressures less than ______________ and toe pressures less than _____________ will be associated with a non-healing wound.

A: 100mmHg, 30mmHg
B: 50mmHg, 30mmHg
C: 50mmHg, 50mmHg
D: 70mmHg, 50mmHg
column2:
Answer: B. 50mmHg, 30mmHg

Feedback:
Rest pain is associated with ankle pressures less than 50mmHg and toe pressures less than 30mmHg will be associated with a non-healing wound.

column1:
Which of the following is related to the appearance of Rouleaux formation on the image?

A: DVT
B: Intracranial collateral flow
C: Thoracic outlet syndrome
D: Raynaud Syndrome
column2:
Answer: A. DVT

Feedback:
Proximal to a DVT the flow may be very slow and visible on 2D imaging; the RBCs appear lined up or stacked in rolls similar to coin rolls from your bank which is called the Rouleaux Formation

column1:
Which of the following procedures allows patients to continue their normal course of anticoagulant therapy before and after the procedure?

A: percutaneous transluminal angioplasty
B: compression therapy of pseudoaneurysm
C: carotid endarterectomy
D: ascending venography
column2:
Answer: D. ascending venography

Feedback:
Venography is performed using a venous puncture. Surgical procedures and invasive procedures that require an arterial puncture have an increased risk of hemorrhage when the blood is thinned by anticoagulants. Patients having these procedures must discontinue their anticoagulant therapy before the procedure. Thinned blood will also inhibit the clot formation in a pseudoaneurysm.

column1:
In most cases, a peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC) line is inserted in the basilic vein exam and advanced until the tip reaches:

A: axillary vein
B: atriocaval junction
C: proximal subclavian vein
D: brachiocephalic vein
column2:
Answer: B. atriocaval junction

Feedback:
The terminal tip of the venous catheter is positioned in a central vein, most commonly the distal 1/3 of the SVC (atriocaval junction). High blood flow volume in this location allows infusates to be rapidly distributed.

column1:
Which of the following is not a patient demographic that is collected to evaluate patient population statistics for those with vascular disease?

A: Gender
B: Age
C: Ethnicity
D: Employment history
column2:
Answer: D. Employment history

Feedback:
Employment history is not considered a relevant patient demographic used to track the occurrence of vascular disease.

column1:
A patient presents with leg pain 2 days after a coronary artery catheterization. All of the following are an important part of the physical evaluation of the patient, except :

A: Evaluation of the leg for signs of pallor
B: Evaluation of the lower leg for loss of hair, dry skin and thick toenails
C: Evaluation of the groin for a bruit
D: Evaluation of the toes for blue discoloration
column2:
Answer: B. Evaluation of the lower leg for loss of hair, dry skin and thick toenails

Feedback:
Loss of hair, dry skin and thick toenails are symptoms of chronic disease that would not really be important to the acute onset of the new symptoms. Angiography procedures have a risk of related pseudoaneurysm, AV fistula (bruit), distal embolization (blue toes) and acute occlusion from the puncture.

column1: Use your mouse to place your cursor over the left renal artery and click to mark the vessel.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The image is called the banana peel view. The aorta is the banana and the renal arteries are "peeling" off the sides of the aorta. The image is obtained with an oblique or coronal view of the abdomen. The anterior image is the right side of the abdomen with the liver and IVC demonstrated closest to the transducer. The right renal artery is the branch of the aorta coursing toward the right side and the transducer (red). The left renal artery is the branch of the aorta coursing away from the right side and the transducer (blue).

column1:
Which of the following would be used to describe a Doppler waveform taken in the mid ICA with a critical stenosis of the proximal segment?

A: increased pulsatility index
B: damped
C: peaked
D: increased resistive index
column2:
Answer: B. damped

Feedback:
The Doppler waveform will be blunted and significantly damped downstream with a significant obstruction proximal to the point of insonation. There will be minimal diastolic flow, but no flow reversal.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes DVT in the lower extremity?

A: Recanalization of a thrombus usually occurs around 5 days after formation occurred.
B: Chronic thrombus will demonstrate complete coaptation of the venous walls upon compression.
C: Acute thrombus will be completely rigid upon compression.
D: Heparin is used to treat a DVT due to its clot lysing properties.
column2:
Answer: A. Recanalization of a thrombus usually occurs around 5 days after formation occurred.

Feedback:
Recanalization of a thrombus usually occurs around 5 days after formation occurred. Heparin is used to treat DVT because it thins the blood and prevents further propagation of the clot. It does not have clot lysing properties. Acute thrombus will be somewhat pliable upon compression. Chronic thrombus will be non-pliable upon compression.

column1:
A normal peak velocity in the cavernosa arteries after a papaverine injection would be _____________

A: <40 cm/s
B: > 30 cm/s
C: <20 cm/s
D: at least 50 cm/s
column2:
Answer: B. > 30 cm/s

Feedback:
A normal peak velocity in the cavernosa arteries after a papaverine injection would be >30cm/s. Reduced velocity indicates vasculogenic impotence.

column1:
Which of the following describes the probe placement for a Doppler evaluation of the frontal artery?

A: near the outer canthus of the eye
B: just above the mid-point of the eyebrow
C: approximately 2cm above the zygomatic arch
D: near the inner canthus of the eye
column2:
Answer: D. near the inner canthus of the eye

Feedback:
Periorbital Doppler is a CW Doppler evaluation of the frontal artery (branch of the ophthalmic artery). An 8-10MHz pencil probe is placed near the inner canthus of the eye. Normal flow direction will be toward the probe.

column1:
What can be done to reduce the spectral broadening on the image?

A: decrease rejection
B: decrease Doppler Gain
C: decrease the PRF
D: decrease wall filter
column2:
Answer: B. decrease Doppler Gain

Feedback:
The image is significantly over-gained. The other choices would only increase the appearance of low frequency reflections/noise.

column1:
What is the best way to adjust the console controls to eliminate the Doppler artifact?

A: raise the baseline
B: increase the sweep speed
C: increase the PW Doppler scale
D: turn down the Doppler gain
column2:
Answer: A. raise the baseline

Feedback:
The current settings have the baseline at 0 at the very bottom of the screen. Extremity arterial flow normally demonstrates antegrade and retrograde flow. The baseline should be raised to demonstrate the flow moving in each direction.

column1:
You identify acute thrombosis of the left popliteal and femoral vein, and the proximal end of the clot has a free-floating tail. What should you do next?

A: Check the great saphenous vein for thrombus propagation
B: Call for a code blue due to the significant increased risk of embolism
C: Check the patient chart for a history of Coumadin therapy
D: Tell the patient to limit all movement because the DVT is unresolved and you need to consult the reading physician immediately
column2:
Answer: C. Check the patient chart for a history of Coumadin therapy

Feedback:
The free floating tail can be a critical finding, The recent history of DVT could indicate that the patient is on anticoagulant therapy. If she is on Coumadin, this greatly reduces the risk of a pulmonary embolism from the tail.. A preliminary report should be given to the nurse and the exam should be read by a physician immediately. A Sonographer should never give results or medical advice to a patient. Code blue is reserved for patients that are not breathing and in cardiac arrest.

column1:
The release of norepinephrine into the blood steam has what effect on the arterioles?

A: decreased flow resistance
B: stenosis
C: vasodilation
D: vasoconstriction
column2:
Answer: D. vasoconstriction

Feedback:
Norepinephrine causes vasoconstriction and adrenaline causes vasodilation.

column1:
If an arteriogram of the carotid arteries indicates a 60% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery, which of the following Doppler velocities from the ICA will correlate well with the predicted level of stenosis?

A: PSV 200cm/s, EDV 80cm/s
B: PSV 300cm/s, EDV 150cm/s
C: PSV 125cm/s, EDV 40cm/s
D: PSV 200cm/s, EDV 160cm/s
column2:
Answer: A. PSV 200cm/s, EDV 80cm/s

Feedback:
Strandness Criteria: 50-79% stenosis = PSV >125cm/s, EDV <140cm/s SRU Criteria 50-69% stenosis = PSV 125 - 230cm/s, EDV 40 - 100cm/s ICAVL Criteria 50-69% stenosis = PSV 180 - 230cm/s, EDV 40 - 100cm/s

column1:
If the blood pressure in the ankle is obtained with the patient's legs in the dependent position:

A: it will be the same as if the patient is evaluated in the supine position
B: the pressure will be falsely elevated
C: the pressure will be underestimated
D: the ankle will demonstrate the same response as if an exercise exam was just performed
column2:
Answer: B. the pressure will be falsely elevated

Feedback:
If the blood pressure in the ankle is obtained with the patient's legs in the dependent position, the pressure will be falsely elevated due to the effects of hydrostatic pressure. Extremity arterial exams should be performed with the patient supine to eliminate the effects of hydrostatic pressure.

column1:
An AV fistula is present connecting the femoral artery and vein at the right groin. The Doppler evaluation of the common femoral artery will demonstrate what flow pattern?

A: peaked, triphasic waveform
B: monophasic with increased diastolic flow
C: biphasic flow with increased diastolic flow reversal
D: monophasic with minimal diastolic flow
column2:
Answer: B. monophasic with increased diastolic flow

Feedback:
A traumatic AV fistula created during an invasive procedure or injury results in the same flow patterns as one created intentionally for dialysis. The low resistance venous connection causes decreased resistance and monophasic flow in the inflow artery proximal to the fistula.

column1:
Diastolic flow reversal is normal in which of the following vessels?

A: internal carotid artery
B: renal arteries
C: celiac artery
D: common iliac artery
column2:
Answer: D. common iliac artery

Feedback:
The lower extremities are normally a high resistance vascular bed. This leads to diastolic flow reversal and a triphasic or biphasic waveform. Any low resistance vessel with a monophasic waveform has no flow reversal during diastole.

column1:
Which of the following statements is correct regarding intraoperative duplex evaluation during a carotid endarterectomy?

A: The technique is used to assess the immediate flow changes within the vessel undergoing the endarterectomy.
B: Sterile gel is required because a sterile sheath is not used due to potential acoustic artifacts.
C: Due to the increased attenuation of sound caused by air in the extracranial vessels, the lowest probe frequency available is preferred for the exam.
D: intraoperative duplex evaluation cannot be performed during a carotid endarterectomy.
column2:
Answer: A. The technique is used to assess the immediate flow changes within the vessel undergoing the endarterectomy.

Feedback:
The technique is used to assess the immediate flow changes within the vessel undergoing the endarterectomy. The highest probe frequency available is preferred for intraoperative duplex evaluation. The probe is placed in direct contact on the vessel that is being evaluated, so the sterile technique is required, including sterile gel with a sterile sheath protecting the transducer. The vessel should not contain air because it would become a cerebral embolism.

column1:
The image demonstrates a _____________________ aneurysm.

A: Fusiform
B: Dissecting
C: Berry
D: Saccular
column2:
Answer: A. Fusiform

Feedback:
Note the uniform dilatation on the anterior and posterior aspects of the vessel consistent with a fusiform AAA.

column1:
This patient has the greatest risk of what complication?

A: rupture
B: dissection
C: distal embolism
D: DVT
column2:
Answer: C. distal embolism

Feedback:
Distal embolization is the most common complication of peripheral arterial aneurysms. A popliteal aneurysm is diagnosed when the vessel diameter exceeds 1cm.

column1:
What is the diameter stenosis of the vessel on the image?

A: 82%
B: 24%
C: 56%
D: 76%
column2:
Answer: D. 76%

Feedback:
(0.54 - 0.13 / 0.54) X 100% = 0.41 / 0.54 = 0.76 x 100% = 76% There should not be challenging calculations on the registry exam because you will not have access to a calculator. The importance of this question is to know how to make the calculation.

column1:
Which vein(s) originates at the ankle and courses along the medial border of the fibula to join the posterior tibial veins just distal to the popliteal fossa?

A: peroneal veins
B: great saphenous vein
C: deep femoral veins
D: small saphenous vein
column2:
Answer: A. peroneal veins

Feedback:
The peroneal vein originates at the ankle and courses along the medial border of the fibula to join the PTV just distal to the popliteal fossa.

column1:
You are performing a left lower extremity venous duplex exam. DVT is identified in the popliteal and femoral veins. Which of the following must also be included in the exam protocol for this patient?

A: ankle brachial index
B: flow in the contralateral common femoral vein
C: pulse volume recording of the calf and thigh of the contralateral leg
D: arterial flow pattern in the arteries associated with the thrombosed veins
column2:
Answer: B. flow in the contralateral common femoral vein

Feedback:
For all unilateral extremity venous exams, the flow in the contralateral extremity should be documented for comparison. Common femoral vein for the lower extremities and subclavian or internal jugular vein for the upper extremities.

column1:
Bilateral leg and foot edema are present. What is the most common cause for this?

A: Chronic insufficiency in deep system
B: Chronic insufficiency in the superficial system
C: Lymphedema
D: Congestive heart failure
column2:
Answer: D. Congestive heart failure

Feedback:
Cardiac and systemic problems lead to bilateral swelling of the lower extremities, including the feet. Lymphedema can cause bilateral leg and foot swelling, but it is usually unilateral and less common than CHF. Chronic venous disease does not normally cause swelling of the feet and is most commonly unilateral.

column1:
A patient presents with left arm paresthesia and intermittent blurring of vision in the right eye. These clinical findings are most consistent with:

A: RT ICA stenosis
B: LT ICA stenosis
C: RT PCA stenosis
D: LT PCA stenosis
column2:
Answer: A. RT ICA stenosis

Feedback:
Extremity paresthesia can be a sign of ICA stenosis. The right ICA feeds the part of the brain that controls the left side of the body. Visual symptoms can be sign of ICA stenosis. The ophthalmic artery is a branch of the ICA and supplies the eye with blood. If the right ICA is blocked, it will affect flow in the right ophthalmic artery and right eye.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes normal results for an air plethysmography (APG) exam?

A: high ejection fraction and residual venous fraction with low venous filling index
B: low ejection fraction and low residual venous fraction with high venous filling index
C: low ejection fraction, low residual venous fraction and venous filling index
D: high ejection fraction, with low residual venous fraction and venous filling index
column2:
Answer: D. high ejection fraction, with low residual venous fraction and venous filling index

Feedback:
Most blood should be ejected out of the leg with calf contraction (high EF%) The VFI is the rate of venous filling. Slower fill rates indicate normal veins. If the VFI is small, that indicates reflux refilling the veins quickly. Residual Venous Fraction is the % of venous volume remaining after tiptoe exercises. Low RVF% of blood should remain in the leg with calf contraction. Higher RVF% values indicate reflux occurred during exercise.

column1:
When you reduce angle correction to make the incident beam more parallel to the blood flow, how does the peak velocity displayed change?

A: decrease
B: no change unless the patient is hypertensive
C: no change unless stenosis is present
D: increase
column2:
Answer: D. increase

Feedback:
As the angle correction is used to make the beam more parallel to the blood flow, the angle between the beam and the flow decreases. As the angle between the beam and the flow decreases, the Doppler shift increases (the more aligned the cursor is to the flow direction, the higher the Doppler shift).

column1:
A diabetic patient presents with an injury to his big toe that won't seem to heal and has been open for over 3 weeks. Which of the following exam techniques should be used to evaluate this patient?

A: segmental pressure exam
B: digital pressures and PPG evaluation
C: treadmill testing
D: cold sensitivity testing
column2:
Answer: B. digital pressures and PPG evaluation

Feedback:
Digital pressures are more accurate in evaluating flow in the legs/feet than the ABI due to medial calcification of the calf/ankle vessels.

column1:
A velocity is recorded in the distal femoral artery of 2.75m/s, what hemodynamic changes will be seen in popliteal artery?

A: increased diastolic flow reversal
B: decreased acceleration time
C: high velocity, peaked waveform
D: increased acceleration time
column2:
Answer: D. increased acceleration time

Feedback:
The resistance distal to the stenosis will be decreased and flow will become monophasic. Acceleration time is prolonged with lower resistance flow.

column1:
Hyperostosis is a limitation of which exam?

A: renal artery Doppler exam
B: lower extremity arterial Doppler exam
C: lower extremity venous Doppler exam
D: transcranial Doppler exam
column2:
Answer: D. transcranial Doppler exam

Feedback:
Hyperostosis refers to the thickening of bones that is most commonly seen in women and older patients. The thicker bones limit sound penetration during a transcranial duplex exam.

column1:
A patient presents for a carotid ultrasound with a history of myocardial infarction and a left ventricular assist device is present. Which of the following describes the expected flow pattern in the normal common carotid artery?

A: low-velocity flow with delayed upstroke and loss of pulsatility
B: high-velocity flow with accelerated upstroke
C: antegrade flow in systole and retrograde flow in diastole
D: triphasic waveform with increased systolic velocity
column2:
Answer: A. low-velocity flow with delayed upstroke and loss of pulsatility

Feedback:
Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD) causes CCA Doppler waveforms with low-velocity flow, delayed upstroke and loss of pulsatility. Carotid Doppler waveform morphology is highly variable among patients with LVADs and is very dependent on pump settings. It is very important to document the presence of a left ventricular assist device or intra-aortic balloon pump in the preliminary report. The waveform contour cannot be evaluated in these patients.

column1:
A patient presents for penile Doppler and segmental pressure exam due to impotence. The ABI is >1.0 bilaterally. The PBI is 0.8. What should be done next to evaluate this patient?

A: Have the patient perform 5 minutes of exercise on the treadmill at 12% grade
B: Use papaverine to assess for decreased venous outflow
C: Write the results as normal with no explanation of impotence
D: Use papaverine to assess for increased venous outflow
column2:
Answer: D. Use papaverine to assess for increased venous outflow

Feedback:
If the PBI is normal in the initial exam, papaverine can be injected at the base of the penis. If the PBI drops, vasculogenic impotence should be suspected. If venous outflow increases post-injection, this can be another cause of impotence in cases with normal arterial inflow.

column1:
At the start of an arterial Duplex exam, you identify monophasic flow in the left common femoral artery. What should you evaluate next to explain these findings?

A: stop the exam and start a PPG evaluation instead.
B: evaluate the groin for a mass pressing on the common femoral artery bifurcation
C: stop the exam and start a PVR evaluation instead.
D: evaluate proximal arteries feeding the CFA, such as the aorta and iliac bifurcation
column2:
Answer: D. evaluate proximal arteries feeding the CFA, such as the aorta and iliac bifurcation

Feedback:
Monophasic flow in an extremity artery indicates a proximal obstruction. If the CFA demonstrates monophasic flow, you should evaluate the vessels proximal to the CFA for stenosis.

column1:
Which of the following will increase spectral broadening on the image?

A: increased PRF
B: increased sample volume size
C: more laminar flow
D: increased wall filter
column2:
Answer: B. increased sample volume size

Feedback:
Increasing sample volume size on PW Doppler will allow for sampling of more blood cells moving at different velocities. The increase in the number of velocities displayed will cause fill in of the spectral window.

column1:
Unilateral swelling of the lower extremity would be an expected clinical finding with:

A: Congestive heart failure
B: Thrombus in the IVC
C: Left popliteal DVT
D: Chronic kidney disease
column2:
Answer: C. Left popliteal DVT

Feedback:
Bilateral swelling is associated with renal disease, CHF, and IVC thrombosis. Left popliteal DVT would cause unilateral swelling of the left leg.

column1:
The image is a transverse view of the right antecubital fossa. What vessel is indicated by letter B?

A: median cubital vein
B: cephalic vein
C: brachial vein
D: basilic vein
column2:
Answer: D. basilic vein

Feedback:
If the image is of the right arm, the left side of the image corresponds with the lateral right arm. The veins of the arm listed from lateral to medial: cephalic, brachial, basilic

column1:
If a patient has a history of _______________, they have an increased risk for carotid dissection.

A: Raynaud syndrome
B: Marfan syndrome
C: Budd Chiari
D: Thoracic outlet syndrome
column2:
Answer: B. Marfan syndrome

Feedback:
Marfan syndrome is a connective tissue disorder that affects the heart and blood vessels. It is often associated with dilatation and/or aneurysm of the aorta, which can lead to intimal dissection.

column1:
Which of the following transducers would be preferred for a lower extremity saphenous mapping on an average sized patient?

A: 5-7MHz curvilinear
B: 7-10MHz sector
C: 7-10MHz linear
D: 5-7MHz linear
column2:
Answer: C. 7-10MHz linear

Feedback:
Linear arrays are preferred for vascular exams. 5-7MHz transducer are used for DVT evaluation in an average sized patient. 7-10MHz transducers are used for evaluation of the superficial system for insufficiency or vein mapping.

column1:
Which of the following symptoms would be most suggestive of significant disease in the middle cerebral artery(MCA)?

A: Contralateral leg paresis
B: Ipsilateral leg paresis
C: Contralateral face and arm paresis
D: Ipsilateral face and arm paresis
column2:
Answer: C. Contralateral face and arm paresis

Feedback:
The MCA feeds the portions of the brain responsible for the face and arm motion. The right side of the brain controls the motor functions of the contralateral (left) side of the body and vice versa. MCA disease will cause symptoms in the contralateral arms and face.The ACA is responsible for supplying the brain tissue that controls the motor functions of the legs.

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding the signs and symptoms of lower extremity arterial disease?

A: Significant ischemic disease in the leg will lead to pallor with dependency.
B: Significant ischemic disease in the leg will lead to rubor with elevation.
C: Significant ischemic disease in the leg will lead to cyanosis upon elevation.
D: Significant ischemic disease in the leg will lead to pallor upon elevation and rubor with dependency.
column2:
Answer: D. Significant ischemic disease in the leg will lead to pallor upon elevation and rubor with dependency.

Feedback:
Significant ischemic disease in the leg will lead to pallor upon elevation and rubor with dependency. Pallor = white, when the legs are raised, blood flow cannot reach the lower legs due to the significant disease in the arteries, the calf color change is white or pale. Rubor = red, when the legs are placed in a dependent position, they initially turn a shade of red due to damage to the capillaries with peripheral arterial disease, the color will change if the legs are raised again.

column1:
Lower extremity arterial segmental pressure exam results in a post exercise basal pressure recovery time of > 6 minutes which suggests:

A: Popliteal entrapment syndrome
B: Iliac disease
C: Single level stenosis
D: Multi-level stenosis
column2:
Answer: D. Multi-level stenosis

Feedback:
Lower extremity arterial segmental pressure exam resulting in a post exercise basal pressure recovery time of 2 to 6 minutes suggests single level disease. Lower extremity arterial doppler exam resulting in a post exercise basal pressure recovery time greater than 6 minutes suggests multi-level disease.

column1:
If a patient has a history of 40% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery identified on a MRA exam. Which of the following Doppler velocities from the ICA will correlate well with the predicted level of stenosis?

A: PSV 300cm/s, EDV 150cm/s
B: PSV 120cm/s, EDV 35cm/s
C: PSV 200cm/s, EDV 80cm/s
D: PSV 200cm/s, EDV 100cm/s
column2:
Answer: B. PSV 120cm/s, EDV 35cm/s

Feedback:
Strandness Criteria: <50% stenosis = PSV <125cm/s SRU Criteria <50% stenosis = PSV <125cm/s, EDV <40cm/s ICAVL Criteria <50% stenosis = PSV <180cm/s, EDV <40cm/s

column1:
What is the most common cardiac disorder associated with arterial embolism?

A: cardiomyopathy
B: Marfan syndrome
C: atrial fibrillation
D: myocardial infarction
column2:
Answer: C. atrial fibrillation

Feedback:
80-90% of arterial emboli are from a cardiac source. Atrial fibrillation can lead to clot formation in the left atrium. If the clot breaks off, it can travel anywhere in the systemic arterial system. If the foramen ovale is patent, the clot could also travel into the right heart and the pulmonary arteries.

column1:
When evaluating the GSV for use as a bypass graft, what is the preferred diameter for the vein?

A: >3mm
B: >10mm
C: >5mm
D: >7mm
column2:
Answer: A. >3mm

Feedback:
When evaluating a vein for use as a bypass graft, a 3mm diameter or greater is preferred.

column1:
Which of the following veins normally do not demonstrate spontaneous flow on Doppler evaluation?

A: posterior tibial and popliteal veins
B: peroneal and femoral veins
C: posterior tibial, peroneal and great saphenous vein
D: All lower extremity veins should normally demonstrate spontaneous flow on Doppler evaluation. Loss of spontaneity indicates flow stasis.
column2:
Answer: C. posterior tibial, peroneal and great saphenous vein

Feedback:
Calf veins and superficial veins do not normally demonstrate flow without augmentation. (Lack spontaneity) Normally the flow is very low velocity and not easily detected without augmentation.

column1:
Blue Toe Syndrome is a complication typically seen with which of the following?

A: Abdominal aortic aneurysm
B: Deep venous thrombosis
C: Thoracic outlet syndrome
D: Raynaud Phenomenon
column2:
Answer: A. Abdominal aortic aneurysm

Feedback:
Blue toe syndrome is caused by emboli reaching the small digital arteries and causing obstruction/ischemia. The ischemic toe tissue becomes discolored and cyanotic in appearance. AAA formation usually leads to thrombus accumulation in the affected area of the vessel. This thrombus poses a risk of emboli formation and distal ischemia.

column1:
Resistance to flow ______________ as blood travels distally through the abdominal aorta.

A: decreases slightly
B: decreases by 50%
C: increases
D: remains constant
column2:
Answer: C. increases

Feedback:
The proximal portion of the aorta demonstrates lower resistance, when compared with the distal portion. The branches of the upper and mid aorta feed low resistance capillary beds in the organs which affects the aortic flow causing it to be biphasic. The distal aorta branches into vessels that supply muscles and supporting structures of the legs. These tissues provide a much higher resistance to flow than the abdominal organs, causing triphasic flow in the distal aorta.

column1: Where does the first caliper go when measuring the acceleration time on PW Doppler? Use your mouse to position the cursor over the correct part of the 3rd waveform and click to set your answer.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
Acceleration time is measured from the start of systole to the peak systolic velocity. Prolonged acceleration time can indicate stenosis proximal to the location where the waveform was obtained.

column1:
Thromboangiitis obliterans involves inflammation of:

A: all vessel walls with no effect on connective tissue
B: all vessel walls and surrounding connective tissue
C: intimal and media wall
D: intimal wall layer
column2:
Answer: B. all vessel walls and surrounding connective tissue

Feedback:
Thromboangiitis refers to diffuse inflammation of the intimal wall layer. Thromboangiitis obliterans refers to inflammation of all vessel walls and surrounding connective tissue. Buerger disease is a type of thromboangiitis obliterans.

column1:
Which of the following would lead to underestimation of stenosis in the internal carotid artery?

A: Short area of focal stenosis
B: Significant aortic valve stenosis
C: Compensatory flow due to stenosis on other side
D: Elevated heart rate
column2:
Answer: B. Significant aortic valve stenosis

Feedback:
Underestimation of stenosis in the ICA can be seen with: Reduced systemic blood pressure Proximal stenosis - such as significant aortic valve stenosis Long area of focal stenosis Tandem stenosis or eccentric stenosis Critical stenosis (string flow) Reduced cardiac output - CHF, coronary artery disease, dilated cardiomyopathy

column1:
Which of the following conditions would benefit from intravascular ultrasound with repair?

A: AAA and renal artery stenosis
B: cerebral artery aneurysm only
C: AAA only
D: renal artery stenosis only
column2:
Answer: A. AAA and renal artery stenosis

Feedback:
Intravascular ultrasound can be used in the repair of aortic aneurysms or renal stenosis

column1:
Thoracic outlet syndrome occurs when the ________________ is compressed by the _______________.

A: celiac axis, median arcuate ligament of the diaphragm
B: brachial plexus, scalene muscle or cervical rib
C: renal artery, diaphragm crura
D: superior mesenteric artery, median arcuate ligament of the diaphragm
column2:
Answer: B. brachial plexus, scalene muscle or cervical rib

Feedback:
Thoracic outlet syndrome occurs when the brachial plexus is compressed by the scalene muscle or cervical rib. Because the nerves are also compressed with the lymph and blood vessels, numbness and tingling are common symptoms of TOS.

column1:
Which statement best describes the findings on the image?

A: Neointimal hyperplasia has developed around a stent.
B: There is soft atheroma formation within the CCA.
C: There is heterogeneous, complex atheroma formation within the CCA.
D: The image demonstrates and area of recanalization through a thrombus.
column2:
Answer: B. There is soft atheroma formation within the CCA.

Feedback:
The image demonstrates homogeneous, soft atheroma formation within the CCA lumen.

column1:
A 9-year-old presents with chronic fatigue and decreased palpable femoral pulses. The ankle brachial index is performed with the following results. Lt arm 168mmHg Lt ankle 95mmHg Rt arm 174mmHg Rt ankle 86mmHg Which of the following is the most likely explanation of the findings?

A: There is most likely a coarctation of the descending aorta.
B: There is most likely atherosclerotic vascular disease present in both lower extremities.
C: There is most likely a subclavian steal present.
D: The bilateral ABIs are normal.
column2:
Answer: A. There is most likely a coarctation of the descending aorta.

Feedback:
The arm pressures are significantly higher than normal. The leg pressures are lower than normal. The bilateral occurrence indicates an aortic abnormality. A coarctation typically occurs just distal to the left subclavian origin. This causes increased pressures above the coarctation/obstruction = elevated brachial pressures. Pressures will drop distal to the coarctation/obstruction = lower ankle pressures bilaterally.

column1:
The saphenous vein has already been harvested from both legs for CABG surgery. What other vein could be harvested for an additional bypass graft?

A: internal mammary artery or subclavian vein
B: subclavian vein or cephalic vein
C: cephalic vein or internal mammary vein
D: cephalic or basilic vein
column2:
Answer: D. cephalic or basilic vein

Feedback:
Cephalic and basilic veins can be harvested for use in coronary artery bypass graft surgery. The internal mammary artery can also be used as a bypass graft, but the question asks for veins.

column1:
Which of the following describes an abnormal response to proximal augmentation?

A: Flow reversal begins upon release of the augmentation maneuver
B: Long venous refill time
C: Flow reversal begins at the onset of the augmentation maneuver
D: No flow reversal occurs with proximal augmentation, but will occur with distal augmentation
column2:
Answer: C. Flow reversal begins at the onset of the augmentation maneuver

Feedback:
If the proximal thigh is squeezed while evaluating the distal GSV, flow reversal will occur as soon as you start to squeeze. The Valsalva maneuver can also be used for proximal augmentation. Flow reversal begins at the start of the Valsalva strain.

column1:
When preparing the sterile tray for an invasive procedure, the cover on the tray should be opened:

A: by the radiologist
B: no more than 2 hours before the procedure begins
C: by pulling the far corner of the wrapper toward you
D: while wearing sterile gloves
column2:
Answer: C. by pulling the far corner of the wrapper toward you

Feedback:
The Sonographer should prepare the supplies for an ultrasound guided procedure. The tray should not be opened until just before the procedure begins. The cover can be opened without wearing gloves but should be pulled toward you to expose the products facing away from you. This prevents your arm from hovering over the supplies to prevent any "debris" from your person from falling into the tray. Sterile gloves must be used to remove items for the tray to prepare them (lidocaine, needle and syringe).

column1:
If new research shows that the minimum velocity criteria used to diagnose >60% renal artery stenosis is actually 225cm/s, not 180cm/s, how will this affect the negative predictive value of the exam?

A: no effect on the negative predictive value, only affects accuracy
B: decrease by 4.5%
C: increase by 4.5%
D: decrease will vary with patient population
column2:
Answer: D. decrease will vary with patient population

Feedback:
Negative predictive value refers to the ability of an exam to correctly predict the absence of disease. Raising the peak velocity required to identify a significant renal stenosis will decrease the negative predictive value. This occurs due to the increased number of patients that have a lower velocity but truly demonstrate >60% stenosis in the renal artery. The positive predictive value will increase for the renal Doppler exam because more patients will actually have significant stenosis when their exam produces velocities above 225cm/s.

column1:
Which of the following is not normally a branch of the ophthalmic artery?

A: supraorbital artery
B: frontal artery
C: posterior auricular artery
D: nasal artery
column2:
Answer: C. posterior auricular artery

Feedback:
The posterior auricular artery branches from the ECA and supplies the ear with oxygenated blood.

column1:
How do you test the capillary blush response on the upper extremity?

A: Compress the radial and ulnar artery simultaneously for 60 seconds to create pallor in the hand and then release to time the return of normal coloring in the hand
B: Apply a blood pressure cuff to the upper arm and inflate above the systolic pressure for 90 seconds then release the cuff to time the return of normal coloring in the arm.
C: Raise the arm above the head for 60 seconds to produce pallor and then place the arm in neutral position to time the return of normal coloring in the arm.
D: Press your finger into the fleshy part of the arm to create pallor, then release to time the return of normal skin coloring.
column2:
Answer: D. Press your finger into the fleshy part of the arm to create pallor, then release to time the return of normal skin coloring.

Feedback:
Press your finger into the fleshy part of the arm to create pallor, then release to time the return of normal skin coloring. Longer times indicate the presence of arterial disease or obstruction.

column1:
What blood vessels are located at letter B?

A: 1 ATA and 2 ATV
B: 2 peroneal arteries and 1 peroneal vein
C: 2 peroneal veins and 1 peroneal artery
D: 1 PTA and 2 PTV
column2:
Answer: C. 2 peroneal veins and 1 peroneal artery

Feedback:

column1:
Which of the following is a contraindication for a lower extremity segmental pressure exam?

A: current anticoagulant treatment
B: ulceration on the 3rd and 4th toes
C: diabetes mellitus
D: ulceration on the distal calf
column2:
Answer: D. ulceration on the distal calf

Feedback:
Contraindications: Suspected or known acute deep venous thrombosis - use waveform analysis only or use of a toe/brachial index (TBI) Recent surgery, trauma site or ulcers that cannot/should not be compressed by pressure cuffs Patients with incompressible arteries due to medial calcification - TBI may be used instead Morbidly obese patients - high-thigh pressure may be unobtainable or inaccurate due to limb girth Patients with significant tremors or involuntary movements that may cause waveforms to be suboptimal or unreliable Ulcerations on the 3rd and 4th toes would not be compressed by cuffs. While diabetes mellitus can cause medial calcification which could inhibit the exam, not every patient with DM has medial calcification.

column1:
Which of the following normal vessels will collapse when you ask the patient to sniff quickly with moderate exertion?

A: common femoral vein
B: basilic vein
C: subclavian vein
D: cephalic vein
column2:
Answer: C. subclavian vein

Feedback:
The quick change in intrathoracic pressure with a sniff should cause the normal subclavian vein to collapse quickly due to the increased outflow into the innominate vein. The cephalic vein is a superficial vein that courses outside the musculature of the arm to enter the subclavian vein at the shoulder. A quick sniff would not affect the flow in a superficial vein. The common femoral vein is outside the chest/abdomen cavity. The change in pressure in these two areas with a quick sniff would not affect a vessel in the extremity.

column1:
A patient is scheduled for a stab phlebectomy. What is being treated for this patient?

A: DVT removal
B: carotid stenosis
C: varicose veins
D: pseudoaneurysm
column2:
Answer: C. varicose veins

Feedback:
Stab Phlebectomy This method removes superficial varicose veins that are not amendable to endovenous ablation. Under local anesthesia, tiny punctures, or microincisions are made. Through these small incisions, phlebectomy hooks extract the varicose veins. The incisions are so small that no stiches are required. Ultrasound guidance can be used to guide the procedure. This procedure is well tolerated by patients and produces excellent cosmetic results.

column1:
The common femoral artery velocity is 0.8 m/s and the proximal femoral artery demonstrates atherosclerosis formation causing an increase in the velocity to 2.2 m/s. This indicates what level of stenosis?

A: 25%
B: 50%
C: 75%
D: 80-99%
column2:
Answer: B. 50%

Feedback:
When the velocity between two extremity arterial segments doubles, this indicates at least a 50% stenosis. When the velocity between two extremity arterial segments quadruples, this indicates at least a 75% stenosis.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes myointimal hyperplasia in an arterial bypass graft?

A: Easily differentiated from atheroma formation
B: Most commonly occurs in an in-situ vein graft at sites of valve removal
C: Not identified on ultrasound until greater than 3 years post-op
D: Caused by the healing process at the anastomosis sites
column2:
Answer: D. Caused by the healing process at the anastomosis sites

Feedback:
Myointimal hyperplasia is a common cause of stenosis at the anastomosis sites of a graft. Native tissues proliferate during the healing process after graft placement. It can be identified anytime from 1 month to 2 years post-op. Difficult to differentiate from soft atheroma formation.

column1:
A patient presents with a radiocephalic hemodialysis fistula in the left arm. Doppler evaluation of which of the following vessels will be used to rule out steal syndrome?

A: Distal inflow artery, within 2cm below the fistula
B: Proximal inflow artery, within 2cm above the fistula
C: Cephalic vein at the shoulder
D: Cephalic vein at the wrist
column2:
Answer: A. Distal inflow artery, within 2cm below the fistula

Feedback:
Doppler evaluation of the inflow artery < 2cm distal to the fistula is used to diagnose steal syndrome. If flow is moving retrograde in the distal radial (inflow) artery, then flow is moving toward the hand in the ulnar artery , through the palmar arch and then toward the upper arm in the radial artery . Normal flow in the radial and ulnar arteries should move toward the hand and into the palmar arch.

column1:
Which statement below is true regarding the normal CCA Doppler tracing?

A: The waveform is a combination of external and internal carotid characteristics with high resistance peaks and increased diastolic flow.
B: The waveform exhibits a sharp upstroke and minimal diastolic flow velocities.
C: The waveform exhibits a triphasic pattern of flow.
D: The waveform exhibits a delayed upstroke and sharp diastolic downslope.
column2:
Answer: A. The waveform is a combination of external and internal carotid characteristics with high resistance peaks and increased diastolic flow.

Feedback:
The waveform is a combination of external and internal carotid characteristics with high resistance peaks and increased diastolic flow. The waveform demonstrates a steep upslope which is indicative of a higher resistance waveform. The gradual downslope that never touches the baseline indicates lower resistance in diastole. The waveform is monophasic with one peak above the baseline.

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding the aorta?

A: Suprarenal AAAs normally do not affect the arterial flow to the kidneys.
B: The majority of the aortic branches that lead to abdominal organs are lateral branches.
C: The most common location for an aortic dissection is at the iliac bifurcation.
D: Rupture is the most common complication of an aortic aneurysm.
column2:
Answer: D. Rupture is the most common complication of an aortic aneurysm.

Feedback:
Rupture is the most common complication of an aortic aneurysm. The most common location for an aortic dissection is in the ascending aorta. The majority of the aortic branches that lead to abdominal organs are anterior branches. A suprarenal AAA would slow the flow to the kidneys and can affect their function. Infrarenal AAAs normally do not affect the arterial flow to the kidneys.

column1:
What change in system controls can reduce the appearance of color displayed over vessel walls?

A: decreasing color PRF
B: increasing persistence
C: increasing color threshold
D: decreasing color priority
column2:
Answer: D. decreasing color priority

Feedback:
Color Priority adjusts the threshold setting. It determines if a pixel is displayed as shade of gray, black or white or as a color. Lower threshold settings reduce the display of color (lower color priority); Higher threshold settings display more color (higher color priority); Necessary to minimize color flash/clutter and improper placement of color on vessel walls.

column1:
The small saphenous vein:

A: originates anterior to the lateral malleolus and ascends along the midline aspect of posterior calf
B: originates posterior to the lateral malleolus and ascends along the midline aspect of posterior calf
C: originates posterior to the medial malleolus and ascends along the midline aspect of posterior calf
D: originates immediately posterior to the Achilles' tendon attachment at the heel and ascends along the midline aspect of posterior calf
column2:
Answer: B. originates posterior to the lateral malleolus and ascends along the midline aspect of posterior calf

Feedback:
The small saphenous vein originates posterior to the lateral malleolus and ascends along the midline aspect of posterior calf.

column1:
The displayed abnormality is most consistent with:

A: Embolism formation
B: Soft plaque formation
C: Tumor invasion
D: Thrombus formation
column2:
Answer: D. Thrombus formation

Feedback:
Plaque forms in arterial vessels. An embolism is a piece of moving thrombus. Tumor invasion would disrupt the integrity of the venous wall layers. The image demonstrates thrombus adhered to the lining of the vessel wall.

column1:
This image demonstrates which of the following?

A: Chronic DVT
B: Partial DVT
C: Acute DVT
D: Superficial venous thrombosis
column2:
Answer: C. Acute DVT

Feedback:
Note the engorged vein with mildly echogenic material within the lumen. The vein appears completely obstructed by acute thrombus.

column1:
A patient is referred to the vascular lab to evaluate the placement of a Greenfield filter. Where will you look for this?

A: IVC
B: abdominal aorta
C: right or left groin
D: subclavian or internal jugular vein
column2:
Answer: A. IVC

Feedback:
The filter is used to prevent pulmonary embolism from the lower extremities. The filter is placed into the IVC below the level of the renal veins.

column1:
The most common location of a Baker cyst is:

A: Inguinal canal
B: Medial popliteal fossa
C: Medial antecubital fossa
D: Anterior patellar space
column2:
Answer: B. Medial popliteal fossa

Feedback:
The most common location of a Baker cyst is in the medial popliteal fossa. Most are an incidental finding on a lower extremity exam.

column1:
Which of the following describes collateral flow from the ECA to the ICA on the same side of the body?

A: posterior communicator to the basilar artery
B: superficial temporal artery to the ophthalmic artery
C: lingual artery to the MCA
D: superior thyroidal artery to the ACA
column2:
Answer: B. superficial temporal artery to the ophthalmic artery

Feedback:
Branches of the superficial temporal artery meet with branches of the supraorbital artery. Retrograde flow in the supraorbital artery will enter the ophthalmic artery, reversed flow will be identified in the ophthalmic artery. Flow will be moving back toward the ICA instead of outward toward the eye.

column1:
In photoplethysmography, the sensor uses _________________ to detect ____________________ changes.

A: light, blood volume
B: light, blood pressure
C: pressure cuff, blood pressure
D: thin mercury tubing, volume
column2:
Answer: A. light, blood volume

Feedback:
The prefix photo- refers to light. Plethysmography is a method used to detect changes in blood volumes in the extremities.

column1:
If both carotid waveforms look like the image above, you should check the patient chart to see if they have:

A: an intra-aortic balloon pump
B: severe aortic stenosis
C: constrictive pericarditis
D: subclavian steal
column2:
Answer: A. an intra-aortic balloon pump

Feedback:
IABP assisted waveforms have 2 systolic peaks for each pulse. The first peak indicates the left ventricular contraction. The second peak indicates balloon inflation. EDV cannot be determined in patients with an IABP because flow reversal is commonly seen after balloon deflation. If possible, turn off the IABP during carotid evaluation. If this is not possible, velocity from the first peak should be used as the PSV. Image Credit Adapted from: Ginat, D., Massey, H. T., Bhatt, S., & Dogra, V. S. (2011). Imaging of mechanical cardiac assist devices. Journal of clinical imaging science, 1, 21. doi:10.4103/2156-7514.80373 Copyright-open license

column1:
The posterior communicating arteries connect the:

A: posterior cerebral artery and middle cerebral artery
B: anterior cerebral artery and posterior cerebral artery
C: basilar and posterior cerebral arteries
D: vertebral and basilar arteries
column2:
Answer: A. posterior cerebral artery and middle cerebral artery

Feedback:
The posterior communicating arteries connect the ipsilateral PCA and MCA.

column1:
The ECA is normally ______________ to the ICA.

A: Inferior
B: Superior
C: Lateral
D: Medial
column2:
Answer: D. Medial

Feedback:
The ECA usually courses anteriorly and medially in the neck to feed structures including the face, tongue and scalp. The ICA usually courses posteriorly and laterally to enter the skull and feed the cerebral tissues.

column1:
Which of the following is directly related to the calculated peak Doppler velocity in an artery?

A: vessel radius
B: flow volume
C: Doppler shift frequency
D: sine of the cursor angle
column2:
Answer: C. Doppler shift frequency

Feedback:
The Doppler shift frequency is directly related to the calculated Doppler velocity.

column1:
A patient presents for a renal transplant evaluation. Which of the following native vessels will be evaluated during the exam?

A: external iliac artery and vein
B: segmental artery
C: distal renal artery
D: arcuate arteries
column2:
Answer: A. external iliac artery and vein

Feedback:
The question asks for native vessels, the external iliac artery and vein are the only native vessels normally evaluated on the exam. The other vessels listed are allograft vessels.

column1:
A patient presents for a venous insufficiency exam. The exam demonstrates normal bilateral great and small saphenous veins. The deep system is not identified well in either leg. Both calves have multiple areas of small, clustered veins superficially. What should you review in the patient chart to explain these findings?

A: treatment history for RF ablation of the bilateral GSVs
B: medical history for Paget Schroetter syndrome
C: surgical history for venous ligation of the bilateral GSVs
D: medical history for Klippel Trenaunay Webber syndrome
column2:
Answer: D. medical history for Klippel Trenaunay Webber syndrome

Feedback:
RF ablation would affect a larger segment of the GSV. Klippel Trenaunay Webber syndrome refers to the congenital absence of the deep venous system of the legs. Paget Schroetter syndrome refers to thrombosis of the subclavian vein due to excessive motion, usually related to TOS.

column1:
Paresthesia is defined as:

A: an abnormal skin sensation, such as numbness or tingling
B: loss of vision in one eye for a short period of time
C: dizziness related to change in patient position
D: loss of ability to move an extremity or other body part
column2:
Answer: A. an abnormal skin sensation, such as numbness or tingling

Feedback:
Paralysis is defined as loss of ability to move an extremity or other body part. Paresthesia refers to an abnormal skin sensation, such as numbness or tingling.

column1:
Which of the following terms can be used to describe the normal Doppler waveform in the portal vein?

A: moderately pulsatile flow due to the vessel proximity to the heart
B: blunted with respiratory variation
C: prominent respiratory variation with mild pulsatility
D: mild pulsatility with respiratory variation
column2:
Answer: D. mild pulsatility with respiratory variation

Feedback:
Venous flow in the portal system normally demonstrates mild respiratory variation and pulsatility. The increased thickness of the vessel walls and location in the abdomen prevent significant cardiac effects on the flow pattern. Mildly pulsatile flow is considered normal with a pulsatility index less than 30%. The pulsatility is not considered "cardiac pulsatility" as the variation is due to the normal flow reversal from the hepatic veins into the venous sinusoids that slows portal inflow, not from cardiac motion. As hepatic vein flow reversal increases with congestion, the pulsatility of the portal vein will increase to abnormal levels.

column1:
It is important to wait at least one minute before repeating what measurement?

A: brachial pressure on an arm with a hemodialysis graft
B: volume flow in a hemodialysis graft
C: ankle blood pressure
D: MCA mean velocity
column2:
Answer: C. ankle blood pressure

Feedback:
When you are obtaining a blood pressure for a second time, it is important to wait at least 1 minute between cuff inflations. Pressure results may be inaccurate if the system is not given the opportunity to normalize after the first pressure measurement. NEVER perform a blood pressure assessment on am arm with a hemodialysis graft.

column1:
You complete a segmental pressure evaluation of an aorta-bifemoral arterial bypass graft and are reviewing the prior report. The prior exam demonstrates: Left ABI of 0.95 and the Right ABI of 0.90. The current exam demonstrates: Left ABI of 0.88 and Right ABI of 0.95. How would you report these findings?

A: Abnormal change in the ABI values of both legs
B: Normal right leg, abnormal left leg
C: Normal left leg, abnormal right leg
D: Normal exam today, no significant change in ABI values
column2:
Answer: D. Normal exam today, no significant change in ABI values

Feedback:
An abnormal exam is indicated when the ABI values drop by more than 0.15.

column1:
If a liver transplant patient has an interposition anastomosis, how does this affect your evaluation?

A: the hepatic artery anastomosis will not be able to be visualized because it is located deep within the liver tissue
B: there will be two anastomosis sites in the main portal vein that must be evaluated
C: each hepatic vein is connected to the native IVC separately, so there will be three anastomosis sites in the IVC that must be evaluated
D: there will be two anastomosis sites in the IVC that must be evaluated
column2:
Answer: D. there will be two anastomosis sites in the IVC that must be evaluated

Feedback:
Before beginning the exam, the surgical notes should be reviewed to determine if the patient has a full or partial transplant, a piggyback or interposition anastomosis and note the anastomosis sites for all vasculature. Piggyback - hepatic confluence of transplant liver attached to native IVC; single anastomosis site Interposition - donor IVC segment replaces a segment of the native IVC; two anastomosis sites

column1:
Thrombolytic therapy is used to treat:

A: chronic venous disease
B: chronic deep vein thrombosis
C: acute arterial occlusion caused by thrombus
D: venous insufficiency
column2:
Answer: C. acute arterial occlusion caused by thrombus

Feedback:
Thrombolytic drugs are used in medicine to dissolve blood clots. Thrombolytic therapy is used to treat extensive acute DVT and acute arterial obstruction by thrombus.

column1:
Resistive Index is a ratio utilized to quantify which of the following?

A: phasicity of flow
B: resistance to flow
C: flow velocity
D: turbulence of flow
column2:
Answer: B. resistance to flow

Feedback:
The resistive index compares the systolic and diastolic flow velocities to evaluate tissue resistance to blood flow. Helpful in evaluation of renal parenchyma, liver parenchyma, and transplant evaluation.

column1:
The profunda artery:

A: courses posterior and medial to the femoral artery
B: courses posterior and lateral to the femoral artery
C: supplies blood to the prostate and pelvic organs in a male patient
D: demonstrates low resistance flow compared to the femoral artery
column2:
Answer: B. courses posterior and lateral to the femoral artery

Feedback:
The profunda artery courses posterior and lateral to the femoral artery.

column1:
The arrow indicates what ultrasound phenomenon?

A: Blood flow within the Vasa vasorum
B: String sign
C: Cardiac pulsatility
D: Clutter
column2:
Answer: D. Clutter

Feedback:
Color flow is demonstrated outside the vessel wall due to increased gain settings and decreased filter settings. The machine detects low amplitude reflections from the vessel wall motion caused by blood flow within the lumen. By adjusting the gain, filter and even the velocity scale, this issue can be corrected.

column1:
The most significant complication seen with DVT is _______________________.

A: Incompetent valves
B: Varicose veins
C: Pulmonary embolism
D: Cardiac Tamponade
column2:
Answer: C. Pulmonary embolism

Feedback:
Varicose vein formation is the most common complication of DVT, and pulmonary embolism is the most significant complication of DVT. A pulmonary embolism occurs when a piece of thrombus breaks off and travels to the lungs. This occludes the bronchial artery and leads to the inability of the lungs to replace the oxygen in the blood. 15% of all cases of sudden death are PE related.

column1:
Abdominal Doppler exams are usually best performed with a frequency of _________MHz and Lower Extremity Arterial Duplex exams are usually best performed with a frequency of _____MHz.

A: 2-4MHz, 8-10MHz
B: 5-7MHz, 8-10MHz
C: 1-3MHz, 5-7MHz
D: 2-4MHz, 5-7MHz
column2:
Answer: D. 2-4MHz, 5-7MHz

Feedback:
Abdominal Doppler exams are usually best performed with a frequency of 2-4MHz and Lower Extremity Arterial Duplex exams are usually best performed with a frequency of 5-7MHz.

column1:
Venous PPG tracings are obtained. The right side demonstrates a venous refill time of 14 seconds. The left side demonstrates a refill time of 23 seconds. Which of the following describes the next step in evaluation?

A: A tourniquet will be applied to the right leg at the thigh level and the VRT assessment will be repeated.
B: The results are normal. No further evaluation is necessary.
C: A tourniquet will be applied to both legs at the thigh level and the VRT assessment will be repeated.
D: A tourniquet will be applied to the left leg at the thigh level and the VRT assessment will be repeated.
column2:
Answer: A. A tourniquet will be applied to the right leg at the thigh level and the VRT assessment will be repeated.

Feedback:
The left side demonstrates a normal VRT > 20 seconds. The right leg requires evaluation with a tourniquet. If the VRT returns to a level above 20 seconds, this indicates the insufficiency is in the superficial system. If the VRT remains abnormal with the superficial system "cut off" by the tourniquet, this indicates deep venous system insufficiency.

column1:
The PW Doppler tracing displays what level of ICA stenosis?

A: 0-49%
B: 50-69%
C: 70-99%
D: 100%
column2:
Answer: C. 70-99%

Feedback:
The peak velocity appears to reach the top of the scale at 450cm/s. Each slash mark on the velocity scale indicates 75cm/s. The estimated end diastolic velocity appears to be about 140cm/s. These velocities indicate an 70-99% stenosis.

column1:
Which of the following is least likely to be associated with renal artery stenosis?

A: Fibromuscular dysplasia
B: Scleroderma
C: CREST syndrome
D: May Thurner syndrome
column2:
Answer: D. May Thurner syndrome

Feedback:
May Thurner syndrome involves the compression of the left common iliac vein against the lumbar vertebrae by the overlying right common iliac artery which leads to thrombosis. Scleroderma and CREST syndrome involve systemic sclerosis of multiple vessels. Fibromuscular dysplasia is also associated with stenosis of the renal arteries.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes the Doppler tracing obtained from a hemodialysis graft?

A: There is significant stenosis present at the venous anastomosis
B: There is reduced volume of flow associated with graft failure
C: The flow pattern is normal
D: Stenosis at the proximal anastomosis of the graft is suspected
column2:
Answer: C. The flow pattern is normal

Feedback:
Turbulent flow in the fistula is an expected finding. Velocities below 400 cm/s are considered normal. The arterial inflow is normally low resistance and very turbulent. The outflow vein will demonstrate increased pulsatility and turbulence.

column1:
This patient would benefit the most from what type of exam?

A: Venous duplex exam
B: Renal artery duplex exam
C: Arterial duplex exam
D: Aortic duplex exam
column2:
Answer: C. Arterial duplex exam

Feedback:
The image demonstrates an ulceration on the foot that is dry, crusty, deep and with regular borders, which is consistent with an ulcer caused by arterial ischemia. An arterial duplex exam will evaluate the arterial system of the leg for areas of stenosis or occlusion and allow the surgeon to consider the best type of treatment (bypass or amputation).

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes the specificity of a testing technique?

A: If the test result is positive, specificity is the probability that the patient actually has the disease
B: The ability of a test to rule out disease when it IS NOT present.
C: If f the test result is negative specificity is the probability that the patient does not have disease
D: The ability of a test to detect disease when it IS present.
column2:
Answer: B. The ability of a test to rule out disease when it IS NOT present.

Feedback:
Specificity - The ability of a test to rule out disease when it IS NOT present. The number of truly negative tests performed in the current modality is compared to the negative tests diagnosed by the gold standard test

column1:
Poiseuille's Law states that the ___________________ has the most significant effect on the blood flow in a vessel.

A: vessel length
B: fluid viscosity
C: pressure gradient
D: vessel radius
column2:
Answer: D. vessel radius

Feedback:
Poiseuille's Law demonstrates the relationship between pressure, flow volume and resistance. A small change in vessel radius causes a dramatic change in the blood flow within. Note the equation lists the radius of the blood vessel and it is raised to the 4th power. This is a significant numerical increase (or decrease) with changes to the radius measurement. Tip for these types of questions: Know your equations! memorizing the Bernoulli, Poiseuille's Law, and Doppler equation will be very helpful in answering relationship questions. Characteristics on the top of the division equation are directly related to the outcome number. Characteristics on the bottom of a division equation are indirectly related to the outcome number. For example: Flow volume = Pressure divided by Resistance. Q= P/R As pressure increases, flow volume increases (direct relationship). As resistance increases, flow volume decreases (indirect or inverse relationship).

column1:
What is the primary factor that determines the resistance characteristics of the arterial signal displayed by PW Doppler?

A: Part it is supplying
B: Branch number
C: Origin of blood
D: Vessel course and location
column2:
Answer: A. Part it is supplying

Feedback:
EX: Renal artery is low resistance for a normal kidney. Organs need constant flow throughout the cardiac cycle. The femoral artery is high resistance for a normal leg. Extremities do not need constant flow throughout the cardiac cycle.

column1:
Which of the following system adjustments would increase the size of the spectral window?

A: changing from a 2mm to 4mm sample size
B: changing from a 4mm to 2mm sample size
C: decreasing rejection
D: tortuous vessel
column2:
Answer: B. changing from a 4mm to 2mm sample size

Feedback:
Smaller sample sizes will evaluate fewer red blood cells and demonstrate a narrower range of frequency shifts. Reducing the sample size will display a larger spectral window beneath a narrow range of frequency shifts.

column1:
A patient presents for a follow up exam after a recent endarterectomy that was performed 3 days ago. The intraoperative report indicates a possible shelf lesion in the ICA. How will this be demonstrated on today's exam?

A: <3mm intimal flap demonstrates a false lumen with turbulent flow
B: Hyperechoic plaque in the vessel lumen with an abrupt edge
C: >3mm intimal flap that causes turbulence and increased velocity
D: <3mm intimal flap that causes turbulence and increased velocity
column2:
Answer: B. Hyperechoic plaque in the vessel lumen with an abrupt edge

Feedback:
The carotid artery is lacerated and the atheroma formation is scraped for removal in an endarterectomy. Complications include: intimal flap dissection shelf lesion - residual hyperechoic plaque in the lumen that has an abrupt edge platelet aggregate - Hypoechoic/anechoic material adjacent to vessel wall that causes increased flow velocity

column1:
Which of the following is an expected clinical finding in a patient with a venous stasis ulcer?

A: located at the medial malleolus
B: deep crater in the skin
C: dry and crusty
D: severe pain
column2:
Answer: A. located at the medial malleolus

Feedback:
Venous ulcers are usually mildly painful, oozing, shallow ulcers located at the medial malleolus. Arterial ulcers are usually severely painful, dry and crusty, deep ulcers located at the toes or heel.

column1:
Which of the following is a potential symptom caused by a subclavian steal?

A: unilateral amaurosis fugax
B: syncope
C: pulmonary HTN
D: systemic HTN
column2:
Answer: B. syncope

Feedback:
Vertebrobasilar disease is associated with syncope. Amaurosis fugax is a visual disturbance that can be related to ICA stenosis. HTN is a risk factor for developing vascular disease.

column1:
Which of the following can be used to describe a normal waveform obtained from a CW Doppler evaluation of the femoral artery?

A: low amplitude
B: delayed deceleration
C: rapid upstroke
D: no diastolic flow reversal
column2:
Answer: C. rapid upstroke

Feedback:
Normal waveforms are triphasic with rapid upslope, sharp peak, rapid downslope, a small peak below the baseline representing diastolic flow reversal, followed by a small peak of flow above the baseline. .

column1:
Which of the following veins does not normally contain any functioning valves?

A: Innominate vein
B: Axillary vein
C: Peroneal veins
D: Small saphenous
column2:
Answer: A. Innominate vein

Feedback:
Lower extremity veins that do NOT contain valves = IVC, iliac veins and soleal sinuses; Upper extremity veins that do NOT contain valves = SVC and innominate veins.

column1:
Which of the following is most suggestive of renal transplant rejection?

A: urinary frequency and urgency
B: reduced serum levels of BUN and creatinine
C: hyperbilirubinemia
D: oliguria
column2:
Answer: D. oliguria

Feedback:
Oliguria or anuria are important clinical indications for renal transplant failure. If the kidney is not producing urine, it is failing. Other signs include elevated BUN and creatinine levels, hematuria and new onset of HTN.

column1:
What patient position should be used when mapping the great saphenous vein for harvest?

A: left lateral decubitus
B: reverse Trendelenburg
C: supine
D: Trendelenburg
column2:
Answer: B. reverse Trendelenburg

Feedback:
Place the extremity in a dependent position. Leg: reverse Trendelenburg for the GSV and decubitus for the SSV Arms: supine with arms extended at side at a level lower than the heart or seated position with hand on lap; tourniquets can be applied in cases where the veins are very small

column1:
While performing an arterial Duplex exam, the patient complains that she is cold. What effect could this have on your exam?

A: monophasic flow may be demonstrated in normal arteries
B: decreased ankle pressures
C: increased pulsatility in the vessels evaluated
D: decreased ankle pressures and monophasic flow may be demonstrated in normal arteries
column2:
Answer: C. increased pulsatility in the vessels evaluated

Feedback:
Cold temperatures cause vasoconstriction in the medium to small arteries of the body. This will lead to increased flow resistance and pulsatility of the flow.

column1:
The carotid compression test is used to _______________, while the carotid sinus massage is used to ______________________.

A: increase flow in the vertebral arteries, decrease flow in the vertebral arteries
B: evaluate the presence of collateral flow, reduce the heart rate
C: stimulate TIA symptoms, reduce TIA symptoms
D: reduce the heart rate, evaluate the presence of collateral flow
column2:
Answer: B. evaluate the presence of collateral flow, reduce the heart rate

Feedback:
The carotid compression test is used to evaluate the presence of collateral flow, while the carotid sinus massage is used to reduce the heart rate to diagnose/treat certain arrhythmias. .

column1:
What statement describes a TIA?

A: Stroke symptoms lasting 24 hrs or less
B: Stroke symptoms lasting 2-4 weeks
C: Stroke symptoms lasting 24 hrs - 72 hrs
D: Stroke symptoms lasting 24 hrs - 2 weeks
column2:
Answer: A. Stroke symptoms lasting 24 hrs or less

Feedback:
TIA = Stroke symptoms lasting 24 hrs or less CVA = Stroke symptoms lasting more than 24hrs

column1:
The _______________________ is described as the segment of the vessel located between the distal basilar artery and the origin of the posterior communicator artery.

A: P2 segment of the PCA
B: M3 segment of the MCA
C: M1 segment of the MCA
D: P1 segment of the PCA
column2:
Answer: D. P1 segment of the PCA

Feedback:
The P1 segment is located between the distal basilar artery and the origin of the posterior communicator artery. The P2 segment is located distal to the origin of the posterior communicator artery.

column1:
A recent CT scan demonstrates an acute infarct in the parietal lobe and a new blockage in the proximal left middle cerebral artery. Which of the following is the most likely source for the embolism?

A: Plaque broke away from the wall of the left anterior cerebral artery
B: Plaque broke away from the sclerotic aortic valve
C: Thrombus formation in the left jugular vein
D: Plaque broke away from the wall of the proximal external cerebral artery
column2:
Answer: B. Plaque broke away from the sclerotic aortic valve

Feedback:
A sclerotic aortic valve can send an embolus through the aorta into one of the arch branches. If the embolus enters the left carotid system, it could lodge in the left MCA. Thrombus in the jugular vein would move into the right side of the heart/lungs. The ECA does not supply flow to the MCA, therefore there is no way for the embolism to enter the circle of Willis (without getting stuck in a smaller ECA branch first). Flow in the ACA normally moves away from the MCA. An embolus in the ACA will move into smaller branches toward the front of the brain and eye.

column1:
Which of the following describes the proper technique for evaluating the ACA through the temporal window?

A: Anterior angulation, 50mm depth
B: Midline placement with 50mm depth
C: Posterior angulation with 65mm depth
D: Anterior angulation with 65mm depth
column2:
Answer: D. Anterior angulation with 65mm depth

Feedback:
When using the temporal window, the ACA is evaluated with anterior probe angulation set at a 65mm depth.

column1:
