The weight of the column of blood within the veins when standing is called?

A: Systemic Pressure
B: Hydrostatic Pressure
C: Pulmonary Pressure
D: Transmural Pressure
column2:
Answer: B. Hydrostatic Pressure

Feedback:
The systemic blood pressure is related to the arterial pressures within the body. The pulmonary pressure is related to the pressure within the pulmonary arteries.

column1:
Which setting should be lowered to the minimum level when beginning to evaluate the patient using the orbital window?

A: Doppler gain
B: Output power
C: Velocity scale
D: Wall filter
column2:
Answer: B. Output power

Feedback:
The intensity of the sound beam passed through the eye should be set to the lowest level possible.

column1:
Which of the following arteries is normally only an intracranial vessel?

A: internal carotid artery
B: vertebral artery
C: basilar artery
D: superficial temporal artery
column2:
Answer: C. basilar artery

Feedback:
The basilar artery is formed by the convergence of the left and right vertebral arteries inside the posterior aspect of the base of the skull.

column1:
According to the findings on this arteriogram, which of the following symptoms would be explained by the findings?

A: Left leg paresthesia
B: Isolated visual disturbances in the left eye
C: Left arm paresthesia
D: Right arm paralysis
column2:
Answer: C. Left arm paresthesia

Feedback:
The carotid system appears patent. The left subclavian artery is absent from the image which indicates complete obstruction at the origin, The left vertebral artery normally originates from the proximal subclavian artery and is also absent from the image. Both of these vessels will demonstrate absence of flow on a Duplex exam. The only symptoms listed that would be related to these findings would be related to the left arm.

column1:
Which of the following is not an expected complication in a 3-week post-op liver transplant patient?

A: hepatic artery atherosclerosis
B: hepatic vein thrombosis
C: portal vein thrombosis
D: biloma
column2:
Answer: A. hepatic artery atherosclerosis

Feedback:
Atherosclerosis formation is a chronic process. Thrombosis and biloma formation are acute complications that can be seen with a recent liver transplant.

column1:
A Greenfield filter is used to prevent which of the following?

A: DVT
B: Pulmonary embolism
C: Blue toe syndrome
D: Cardiac tamponade
column2:
Answer: B. Pulmonary embolism

Feedback:
A greenfield filter is inserted into the abdominal portion of the IVC (below the level of the renal veins) to catch embolic material before it reaches the heart and lungs. It does not prevent the formation of DVT, but it does protect the patient from complications related to embolism from a DVT.

column1:
Which of the following requires a Doppler cursor angle of 60 degrees or less for accurate reporting of numerical values?

A: S/D ratio
B: peak systolic velocity
C: resistive index
D: pulsatility index
column2:
Answer: B. peak systolic velocity

Feedback:
The resistive index, pulsatility index and S/D ratios are not dependent on the Doppler cursor angle for accurate calculations. Because the values reported are ratios and not velocities, Doppler angle is not relevant to the results of the calculations. When the peak systolic or diastolic velocities of flow are reported, the Doppler cursor angle must be used to calculate and report accurate values.

column1:
According to the Bernoulli Principle, where will you find the lowest pressure in a vessel with stenosis?

A: proximal to the stenosis
B: at the site of stenosis
C: distal to the stenosis
D: in the collateral vessels
column2:
Answer: B. at the site of stenosis

Feedback:
Pressure is lowest at the stenotic site which causes an increased pressure gradient from proximal to the stenosis to the stenosis site. This increased gradient leads to increased velocity of flow across the stenotic site.

column1:
Which of the following describes the most common flow pattern seen in a Type II endoleak?

A: continuous, phasic flow
B: bidirectional
C: monophasic retrograde flow
D: monophasic, low resistance
column2:
Answer: B. bidirectional

Feedback:
Flow within a Type I leak resembles the flow within the graft. Flow in a Type II leak usually demonstrates a bidirectional flow pattern because the Doppler waveform morphology reflects the end source resistance. A single branch vessel entering the sac will demonstrate bidirectional flow.

column1:
The pulsatility index (PI) in a normal aorta should be ______________ the PI in the normal popliteal artery.

A: higher than
B: at least 3 times greater than
C: very similar to
D: lower than
column2:
Answer: D. lower than

Feedback:
The pulsatility index (PI) in a normal aorta should be lower than the PI in the normal popliteal artery. The aorta flow is lower resistance than the popliteal artery, therefore there is a greater amount of pulsatility in the popliteal artery. The PI increases from the central arteries to the peripheral extremity arteries. A PI <5.0 in a lower extremity artery indicates proximal obstruction.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes a gold standard test?

A: A test that is preferred by most doctors to make a specific diagnosis
B: A type of test that has been used for over 15 years to successfully diagnose rule out disease when it is absent
C: A type of test that has been used for over 15 years to successfully diagnose disease when it is present
D: An accepted test that is assumed to be able to determine the true disease state of a patient, regardless of positive or negative test findings
column2:
Answer: D. An accepted test that is assumed to be able to determine the true disease state of a patient, regardless of positive or negative test findings

Feedback:
Gold Standard Testing refers to an accepted test that is assumed to be able to determine the true disease state of a patient, regardless of positive or negative test findings or sensitivities or specificities of other diagnostic tests used. As new diagnostic methods become available, the "gold standard" test may change over time. For example, an aortogram used to be the gold standard test for the diagnosis of aortic dissection, but today MRA has become the new "gold standard" test for aortic dissection because it has the highest sensitivity and specificity levels.

column1:
Which of the following occurs during calf muscle relaxation?

A: The gastrocnemius veins fill with blood from the popliteal vein
B: blood flows through the perforators from the superficial to the deep veins
C: blood flows through the perforators from the deep to the superficial veins
D: the superficial veins fill with blood
column2:
Answer: B. blood flows through the perforators from the superficial to the deep veins

Feedback:
When the muscle contracts, flow moves from the deep calf veins into the popliteal and femoral vein. As the muscle relaxes, blood is essentially sucked through the perforators from the superficial system into the deep system to play a part in refilling the deep vessels.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes the color displayed on the image?

A: The color scale should be reduced so that the entire vessel is filled with red color.
B: The heel-to-toe method can be used to change the course of the vessel across the image, and this will allow the vessel to accurately display a single color
C: The color scale should be reduced so that the entire vessel is filled with blue color.
D: The red and blue colors indicate that flow is moving in two different directions simultaneously in the vessel
column2:
Answer: B. The heel-to-toe method can be used to change the course of the vessel across the image, and this will allow the vessel to accurately display a single color

Feedback:
Color is displayed according to the direction the flow is moving in relation to the ultrasound beam. If the flow is perpendicular to the beam, no color will be displayed. This color map indicates flow toward the transducer is red and flow moving away is blue. To improve the color display in the image, the transducer should be angled (heel-to - toe) to demonstrate the vessel at a steeper angle across the screen. This will eliminate the area without color display that is perpendicular to the beam. It will also allow all flow in the vessel to be displayed as moving in the same direction.

column1:
A tardus parvus waveform is the result of:

A: significant proximal arterial stenosis
B: <50% proximal arterial stenosis
C: distal arterial obstruction
D: partial venous obstruction
column2:
Answer: A. significant proximal arterial stenosis

Feedback:
A tardus parvus waveform is associated with significant stenosis that severely decreases flow through an artery. This waveform is described as low velocity and resistance with a late systolic peak. EXAMPLE OF TARDUS PARVUS WAVEFORM IN RENAL STENOSIS

column1:
Lymphedema is commonly related to:

A: a history of radiation therapy or cancer
B: peripheral arterial disease
C: a pulmonary embolism
D: Leriche syndrome
column2:
Answer: A. a history of radiation therapy or cancer

Feedback:
Lymphedema occurs due to an obstructed lymphatic system. It can be caused by many things, but is commonly seen with infection, injury, trauma, or cancer that affects the lymphatic system. Radiation therapy or removal of lymph nodes related to cancer can also cause lymphedema.

column1:
Which of the following exams does not use PW or CW Doppler?

A: pulse volume recording
B: extracranial exam
C: transcranial exam
D: periorbital exam
column2:
Answer: A. pulse volume recording

Feedback:
Pulse volume recording is performed using blood pressure cuffs to measure volume changes under the cuffs.

column1:
Which of the following disorders would demonstrate normal digital pressures in the upper extremity?

A: Thoracic outlet syndrome
B: Raynaud disease
C: SVC syndrome
D: Buerger disease
column2:
Answer: C. SVC syndrome

Feedback:
Raynaud disease, TOS, Buerger disease and atherosclerosis can all lead to abnormally low digit pressures and abnormal PPG tracing. SVC Syndrome refers to progressive thrombosis of the SVC that is usually related to adjacent malignant tumor formation.

column1: Use your mouse to place your cursor over the hepatic artery and click to mark the vessel. If the vessel is not demonstrated on the image, mark the purple box that says "Not demonstrated on the image".
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
This image demonstrates the aorta, SMA and the left renal vein. The celiac axis and its branches are cephalad to the level of this image. The SMA is visible anterior to the aorta, therefore the hepatic artery would not be visualized in this plane.

column1:
Which of the following describes the correct way to measure the acceleration time for a renal artery waveform?

A: place a caliper at the onset of the systolic upstroke and at the true systolic peak
B: place a caliper at the onset of the systolic upstroke and at the early systolic peak
C: place a caliper at the true systolic peak and the end diastolic velocity
D: place a caliper at the early systolic peak and the end diastolic velocity
column2:
Answer: B. place a caliper at the onset of the systolic upstroke and at the early systolic peak

Feedback:
Resistive Index - place a caliper at the true systolic peak and the end diastolic velocity; Acceleration Time - place a caliper at the onset of the systolic upstroke and at the early systolic peak

column1:
The image is a transverse view of the distal popliteal fossa. Which of the following would not be a recommended treatment?

A: IV Heparin
B: Lovenox injection
C: No medication, just a warm compress
D: Coumadin therapy
column2:
Answer: C. No medication, just a warm compress

Feedback:
DVT and superficial thrombosis are present. DVT is always treated aggressively with protocols using IV Heparin, Lovenox injection and Coumadin/Warfarin therapy. If only superficial thrombosis of the small saphenous vein is present, a warm compress and an aspirin would usually be sufficient treatment.

column1:
When compared to the normal left CCA, the right CCA demonstrates reduced velocity, slower upstroke and holodiastolic flow. These findings are most consistent with:

A: stenosis proximal to the point of insonation
B: congestive heart failure
C: elevated hematocrit levels
D: decreased hematocrit levels
column2:
Answer: A. stenosis proximal to the point of insonation

Feedback:
If a single CCA demonstrates reduced velocity, slower upstroke and holodiastolic flow = stenosis proximal to the point of insonation. The vessel demonstrates the characteristics of flow distal to a stenosis. The other choices would cause hemodynamic changes in both carotid arteries.

column1:
A segmental pressure exam demonstrates the following pressures: distal thigh 144, calf 166, ankle 132. Which of the following could explain these results?

A: improper cuff placement and incorrect cuff size, but not medial calcification
B: medial calcification or venous insufficiency
C: incorrect cuff size would be the only possible cause
D: incorrect cuff size, medial calcification and improper cuff placement
column2:
Answer: D. incorrect cuff size, medial calcification and improper cuff placement

Feedback:
The calf pressure should not demonstrate an increased pressure related to the thigh pressure. All of the choices can lead to an overestimated pressure measurement.

column1:
Which of the following measurements should be obtained to evaluate the success of a percutaneous angioplasty performed on the left proximal superficial femoral artery?

A: acceleration time of the distal superficial femoral artery
B: evaluate the ABI value for the expected decrease
C: femoral artery diameters proximal to the treated area, the treated area and distal to the treated area
D: obtain waveforms at the PTLA site and measure the peak systolic velocity and pulsatility index
column2:
Answer: D. obtain waveforms at the PTLA site and measure the peak systolic velocity and pulsatility index

Feedback:
Evaluating a patient post-angioplasty of the lower extremity: ABI assessment - greater than 0.9 and/or an increase of at least 0.15 in the ABI from pre-procedure levels Evaluate inflow artery for damping - measure acceleration time <180ms is normal (AT is used to evaluate INFLOW) Evaluate the PTLA site for residual plaque or thrombus Record waveforms at the PTLA site and proximal to it - measure the velocity and pulsatility index Calculate the velocity ratio - less than 2 is normal

column1:
If the great saphenous vein is being used to create an in-situ vein graft,

A: all perforator veins must be marked on the vein mapping so they can be closed
B: the graft diameter will be greatest at the distal end of the graft
C: the valves will remain in place and provide little resistance to arterial flow
D: all perforator veins must be evaluated for patency because the graft will not function properly with obstructed perforator veins
column2:
Answer: A. all perforator veins must be marked on the vein mapping so they can be closed

Feedback:
You must mark all perforating veins if the GSV will be used as an in-situ graft or an AV fistula will be created when the graft is arterialized. The valves must be removed with a valvulotome device. The graft diameter will be greatest at the proximal end.

column1:
Which of the following is NOT normally performed after the injection of papaverine into the tissues proximal to the shaft of the penis:

A: Repeat the ABI measurements
B: Doppler waveforms are obtained in the cavernous arteries and veins 1-2 minutes post-injection
C: Apply the 2.5cm blood pressure cuff around the shaft of the penis
D: Measure the diameter of the cavernous arteries
column2:
Answer: A. Repeat the ABI measurements

Feedback:
When performing a penile Doppler exam: Obtain Doppler samples of the CFA, cavernous arteries and veins in the penis. Doppler samples will also be taken in the PTA and DPA while performing ankle pressures. The ABI should be evaluated to determine if PAD can be causing erectile dysfunction. The diameter of the cavernous arteries should be measured. After the injection the penile artery diameters and velocities are repeated. The penile pressure is obtained using a 2.5cm cuff and the PBI is calculated. The ABI is performed before the injection to assess any PAD that may be present.

column1:
Most commonly the posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis arteries are used to obtain the pressure at the ankle. Which of the following describes the correct calculation of the ankle brachial index?

A: highest brachial pressure divided by the highest ankle pressure on each ankle
B: lowest ankle pressure on each leg divided by the highest brachial pressure
C: highest right ankle pressure divided by the right brachial pressure and highest left ankle pressure divided by the left brachial pressure
D: highest ankle pressure on each leg divided by the highest brachial pressure
column2:
Answer: D. highest ankle pressure on each leg divided by the highest brachial pressure

Feedback:
When calculating the ABI, the highest ankle pressure on each leg is divided by the highest brachial pressure.

column1:
You are evaluating a patient with suspected Raynaud disease using PPG. After obtaining a baseline tracing, you place the hand in cold water for 2 minutes. Once the hand is removed, PPG sensors are reapplied to the fingertips. Which of the following correctly describes normal results for this exam?

A: waveforms return to baseline within 5 minutes after removing the hand from the cold water
B: waveform amplitude increases to at least 4X the original
C: waveforms return to baseline within 7 minutes after removing the hand from the cold water
D: waveform amplitude increases to at least 2X the original
column2:
Answer: A. waveforms return to baseline within 5 minutes after removing the hand from the cold water

Feedback:
Cold sensitivity testing is used to evaluate suspected Raynaud disease. The cold water elicits vasoconstriction. A normal exam would demonstrate PPG tracings that return to baseline levels within 5 minutes after the hand is removed from the cold water. An abnormal exam would have a delayed return to baseline readings.

column1:
What vessel(s) should be closely evaluated for a related cause for a new cerebellar infarct seen on a CT of the brain?

A: anterior and middle cerebral arteries
B: posterior cerebral artery and carotid siphon
C: middle and posterior cerebral artery
D: vertebral and basilar arteries
column2:
Answer: D. vertebral and basilar arteries

Feedback:
The cerebellum is supplied by branches from the vertebral and basilar arteries. A cerebellar infarct would by related abnormalities of the vertebrobasilar circulation.

column1:
Patients are examined in the ______________ position for a lower extremity arterial Doppler exam in order to _______________.

A: semi-erect, increase hydrostatic pressure
B: semi-erect, reduce hydrostatic pressure
C: supine, increase hydrostatic pressure
D: supine, reduce hydrostatic pressure
column2:
Answer: D. supine, reduce hydrostatic pressure

Feedback:
Patients are examined in the supine position for a lower extremity arterial Doppler exam in order to reduce hydrostatic pressure.

column1:
What can be done to inhibit light from the exam room from causing artifact on an upper extremity PPG tracing?

A: perform the exam with all lights off and windows covered
B: cover the patient's hand with a towel or sheet
C: place acoustic gel between the PPG sensor and the skin
D: increase the room temperature
column2:
Answer: B. cover the patient's hand with a towel or sheet

Feedback:
To reduce light from the exam room from causing artifact on the PPG tracing, cover the patient's hand with a towel or sheet.

column1:
Which of the following is true regarding compression techniques used to treat a pseudoaneurysm of the femoral artery in the groin?

A: Blood pressure evaluation cannot be performed anywhere on the legs until the stalk to the pseudoaneurysm is closed.
B: Pressure equivalent to 100mmHg should be performed in three ten-minute intervals and if this does not close the stalk, alternative treatment must be considered
C: Thrombin injection is more effective than compression techniques in pseudoaneurysms with large diameter stalks.
D: Compression should be performed in 30 second intervals with color Doppler evaluation in between compressions.
column2:
Answer: C. Thrombin injection is more effective than compression techniques in pseudoaneurysms with large diameter stalks.

Feedback:
Thrombin injection is more effective than compression techniques in pseudoaneurysms with large diameter stalks. Ankle pressure should be monitored during compression techniques to avoid changes in distal flow from extrinsic compression. Compression should be performed in 10 - one minute intervals with color Doppler evaluation in between compressions.

column1:
__________________ refers to when the carotid artery is lacerated and the atheroma formation is scraped for removal.

A: An endarterectomy procedure
B: An atherectomy procedure
C: An angioplasty procedure
D: A valvulotome procedure
column2:
Answer: A. An endarterectomy procedure

Feedback:
Atherectomy uses a catheter with a rotating head and suction capabilities to break up the plaque and remove it from the artery. Angioplasty uses a balloon tipped catheter to compress the atheroma and expand the vessel lumen. An endarterectomy refers to when the carotid artery is lacerated, and the atheroma formation is scraped for removal. A valvulotome procedure is used to remove valves from inside a vein.

column1:
What is the proper procedure for removing soiled gloves?

A: Pull the first glove off using the fingertip area, while keeping the dirty side out, and dispose of it. Repeat the process for the second glove.
B: Using sterile scissors, cut the glove across the palm of the hand, peel it off and dispose. Repeat the process for the second glove.
C: Using sterile scissors, cut the glove across the back of the hand, peel it off and dispose. Repeat the process for the second glove.
D: Pull the outer cuff of the first glove toward the fingers while turning it inside out. Place the removed glove in the contralateral gloved hand and pull the outer cuff of the second glove toward the fingers while turning it inside out and enclosing the first glove.
column2:
Answer: D. Pull the outer cuff of the first glove toward the fingers while turning it inside out. Place the removed glove in the contralateral gloved hand and pull the outer cuff of the second glove toward the fingers while turning it inside out and enclosing the first glove.

Feedback:
When removing used/soiled gloves, proper disposal is important for infection control. Pull the outer cuff of the first glove toward the fingers while turning it inside out. Place the removed glove in the contralateral gloved hand and pull the outer cuff of the second glove toward the fingers while turning it inside out and enclosing the first glove. The end result should be a "ball" of gloves with the 1st glove trapped inside the second and the inside of the gloves exposed (not the outside) when disposing of them.

column1:
If the velocities in both carotid arteries are increased since last year's exam with no visible change in plaque formation, what should you review in the patient chart as a possible explanation?

A: lab values for decreased hematocrit levels
B: medical history for polycythemia vera
C: recent echocardiogram for signs of decreased cardiac output
D: medical history for controlled HTN
column2:
Answer: A. lab values for decreased hematocrit levels

Feedback:
Decreased hematocrit will lead to thinned blood with increased bilateral carotid velocities. Polycythemia vera leads to thickening of the blood which would decrease carotid velocities bilaterally. Increased output and uncontrolled HTN would cause increased bilateral carotid velocities.

column1:
When evaluating heavily calcified arteries, what system settings should be increased to improve the demonstration of flow in the vessels?

A: color gain and angle of insonation
B: persistence and sensitivity
C: velocity scale and persistence
D: frequency and output power
column2:
Answer: B. persistence and sensitivity

Feedback:
Arterial calcification can limit the 2D and color Doppler evaluation. To evaluate heavily calcified arteries, use multiple imaging planes, increase color Doppler gain, increase sensitivity and persistence levels. These changes in system settings should increase the amount of color displayed.

column1:
________________________ aids in measurement of the acceleration time of the renal arteries.

A: Reducing rejection
B: Increasing the sweep speed
C: Using a lower frequency transducer
D: Increasing the PRF
column2:
Answer: B. Increasing the sweep speed

Feedback:
Faster sweep speeds produce wider waveforms on the display to facilitate easier and more accurate measurement of the AT.

column1:
Increasing the spectral Doppler sample size will increase the ___________________.

A: spectral broadening
B: size of the spectral window
C: sweep speed
D: Doppler shift
column2:
Answer: A. spectral broadening

Feedback:
Smaller Doppler sample volumes will produce a waveform with a larger spectral window because the smaller volume of cells sampled are moving at similar speeds. Larger Doppler sample volumes will produce a waveform with a small or absent spectral window because the larger volume of cells sampled are moving at varied speeds.

column1:
What is indicated by the red arrows on this subcostal image?

A: Portal Vein
B: Abdominal Aorta
C: Mirror image artifact
D: IVC
column2:
Answer: C. Mirror image artifact

Feedback:
The connective tissue is very reflective which causes a mirror image of the heart, IVC and liver tissue.

column1:
If the physician is having trouble visualizing the needle on the ultrasound screen during a thrombin injection, how can you help to improve the visualization of the needle?

A: use electronic steering to reduce the incident angle between the US beam and the long axis needle to a value closer to 0 degrees
B: use a lower frequency probe
C: use electronic steering to increase the incident angle between the US beam and the long axis needle to a value closer to 90 degrees
D: increase the output power to the maximum setting
column2:
Answer: C. use electronic steering to increase the incident angle between the US beam and the long axis needle to a value closer to 90 degrees

Feedback:
The more perpendicular the US beam is to the needle, the better the reflection properties and visibility on the screen.
